content.text: FastAPI is a modern Python web framework for building HTTP APIs with automatic OpenAPI documentation, Pydantic-based validation, and native async support. For agentic AI systems, it serves as the HTTP backbone — exposing agent invocation endpoints, streaming LLM tokens to clients, and accepting tool callbacks — without the threading limits of WSGI frameworks like Flask.
FastAPI is a Python 3.8+ web framework built on

(runtime type validation). It turns Python type annotations directly into OpenAPI schemas, request validators, and response serialisers. The core idea: declare what your endpoint expects and returns using Python type hints, and FastAPI handles HTTP parsing, docs generation, and error messages for free.

FastAPI positions itself between two extremes: Flask (simple, sync, minimal) and Django REST Framework (batteries-included, heavy). It targets the growing class of ML and AI services that need async I/O, streaming responses, and structured JSON interfaces.

The name "Fast" refers to two things: runtime performance (comparable to NodeJS and Go for I/O-bound workloads, thanks to asyncio) and development speed (type-driven code generation reduces boilerplate dramatically).
Agent backends require three capabilities that older Python frameworks struggle with:
— token-by-token Server-Sent Events (SSE) or chunked HTTP require true async streaming, not buffered responses.
High concurrency with low I/O overhead
— an agent may fan out to five external tool calls simultaneously. Async I/O handles all five in one event loop; threads waste RAM at scale.
— when agent tools call your service, a correct OpenAPI schema lets orchestrators discover capabilities automatically.
— Pydantic models give you free input sanitisation, type coercion, and rich error messages — critical for preventing prompt-injection via malformed tool inputs.
FastAPI became the default choice for production AI APIs in 2023–2025. Tools like LangServe, Modal, and Anthropic's Claude API SDKs all expose or recommend FastAPI-compatible interfaces.
generated from type hints at zero extra cost.
— request bodies validated and parsed before your handler runs; 422 Unprocessable Entity returned automatically on schema mismatch.
handlers run on uvicorn's asyncio event loop;
chains composable dependencies (auth checks, DB sessions, shared clients) without globals.
— return a Pydantic model and FastAPI serialises it; mismatches surface at startup, not in production.
— built-in WebSocket support on the same ASGI server for real-time agent-to-UI communication.
lifecycle manages shared connections opened once at startup.
, synchronous ORM) blocks the entire event loop and degrades all concurrent requests.
— FastAPI 0.100+ uses Pydantic v2 which has breaking API changes; third-party plugin compatibility lags.
— no built-in ORM, auth system, or admin. Every choice (SQLAlchemy vs Tortoise, JWT vs OAuth2) is your own.
— Pydantic model compilation and OpenAPI generation add cold-start latency (typically 0.5–2s) that matters in serverless contexts.
— fewer battle-tested middleware packages than Flask/Django, though the gap has closed significantly.
A request enters uvicorn, which implements the ASGI spec. Uvicorn calls FastAPI's
coroutine. FastAPI's internal Starlette router matches the URL path and HTTP method, invoking the registered handler. Before the handler body executes, FastAPI resolves
dependencies, validates path/query parameters, and parses the JSON body into Pydantic models. The handler runs, the return value is serialised, and the ASGI
coroutine pushes bytes back to the client.
When you annotate a handler parameter with a Pydantic model, FastAPI reads the model's field definitions and registers them as the JSON schema for that endpoint. On each request, FastAPI calls
(Pydantic v2) which applies type coercions and validators. If validation fails, FastAPI catches
and returns a 422 response with structured error detail — no handler code needed.
is a recursive resolver. When a handler parameter is declared as
(which can itself have dependencies), caches the result for the request's lifetime, and passes it to the handler. Generator dependencies (using
) automatically clean up after the response is sent.
— expose agent invocation endpoints that clients call to trigger agent runs.
with an async generator yielding SSE tokens.
— MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers and custom tool APIs where structured JSON schemas must match exactly.
— services that fan out to multiple LLM or external API calls concurrently.
— accept callbacks from Slack, GitHub, Stripe with Pydantic-validated payloads.
— services calling each other with httpx AsyncClient; FastAPI + uvicorn is fast and lean.

Avoid FastAPI when you need a heavy admin panel (prefer Django), when your codebase is entirely synchronous and migrating would be disruptive, or when you are building a CLI-only agent that never serves HTTP.

pip install fastapi uvicorn[standard] httpx pydantic
# uvicorn[standard] includes websockets, uvloop (Linux), httptools

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from typing import AsyncIterator
import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException, BackgroundTasks
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


# --- Shared async HTTP client via lifespan ---

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI) -> AsyncIterator[None]:
    """Open shared resources on startup; close on shutdown."""
    app.state.http = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=60.0)
    yield
    await app.state.http.aclose()


app = FastAPI(title="AgentAPI", version="1.0.0", lifespan=lifespan)


# --- Pydantic models ---

class AgentRequest(BaseModel):
    prompt: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=8192)
    max_tokens: int = Field(default=1024, ge=1, le=4096)
    stream: bool = False


class AgentResponse(BaseModel):
    run_id: str
    output: str
    tokens_used: int


# --- Dependency: verify API key ---

async def verify_key(x_api_key: str | None = None) -> str:
    if x_api_key != "secret-key":  # swap for real auth
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid API key")
    return x_api_key


# --- Streaming endpoint ---

@app.post("/agent/stream")
async def stream_agent(req: AgentRequest, _key: str = Depends(verify_key)):
    """Stream LLM tokens as Server-Sent Events."""

    async def token_generator():
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            async with client.stream(
                "POST",
                "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages",
                headers={"x-api-key": "YOUR_KEY", "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01"},
                json={
                    "model": "claude-opus-4-5",
                    "max_tokens": req.max_tokens,
                    "stream": True,
                    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": req.prompt}],
                },
            ) as resp:
                async for line in resp.aiter_lines():
                    if line.startswith("data:"):
                        yield f"{line}\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(token_generator(), media_type="text/event-stream")


# --- Sync-style (non-streaming) endpoint ---

@app.post("/agent/run", response_model=AgentResponse)
async def run_agent(
    req: AgentRequest,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
    _key: str = Depends(verify_key),
) -> AgentResponse:
    """Run agent and return full response."""
    import uuid, asyncio
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    # Simulate async LLM call
    await asyncio.sleep(0)
    background_tasks.add_task(log_run, run_id, req.prompt)
    return AgentResponse(run_id=run_id, output=f"Echo: {req.prompt}", tokens_used=42)


async def log_run(run_id: str, prompt: str) -> None:
    print(f"[log] run_id={run_id} prompt_len={len(prompt)}")

# routers/agent.py
from fastapi import APIRouter
router = APIRouter(prefix="/agent", tags=["agent"])

@router.post("/run")
async def run(): ...

# main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from routers import agent

app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(agent.router)
# Auto-namespaced: POST /agent/run

A production agentic-AI platform serves 200 concurrent users. Each agent run calls three external APIs (web search, vector DB, LLM). Under Flask+Gunicorn (8 workers), 8 concurrent runs exhaust all workers — additional requests queue. Under FastAPI+Uvicorn (4 workers, 4 cores), each worker's event loop handles 50+ concurrent awaits, so 200 simultaneous runs comfortably fit in 4 workers with asyncio scheduling gaps between await points. Streaming LLM output (Server-Sent Events) works natively; the Flask version required a separate Celery+Redis pipeline for the same feature.
handler freezes all concurrent requests. Use
on a coroutine call silently returns the coroutine object, not the result.
— a global dict mutated across concurrent handlers can corrupt under asyncio scheduling. Use asyncio.Lock or a database.
Opening a new httpx client per request
— creating and destroying an AsyncClient per request wastes connection setup. Create one in lifespan and inject it.
Using sync ORM in async handlers
— SQLAlchemy 1.x sync sessions block the event loop. Use SQLAlchemy 2.x async sessions or asyncpg.
— bypasses Pydantic serialisation and can leak internal fields. Always define response_model.
— clients that silently drop 422 errors miss validation failures. Log and alert on 4xx at the API gateway.
— Pydantic v1 and v2 have incompatible validator APIs; unpin and a CI upgrade can break all models silently.
, DB pools, and loaded ML models belong in startup, not at module level.
— one router per domain (agent, tools, health); include with a prefix. Keeps
— explicit response types enable docs generation and prevent accidental data leaks.
keeps constraints co-located with the type declaration.
— no running server needed for unit tests (see Testing section).
— inject a UUID per request via middleware; include in every log line for traceability.
— lifespan teardown ensures in-flight async operations complete before the server exits.

FastAPI uses Python's asyncio event loop. A single uvicorn worker can handle thousands of concurrent connections if each awaitable releases the event loop (e.g., waiting on network I/O). The rule: any
point is an opportunity for another coroutine to run. CPU-bound work (embedding generation, image processing) must be offloaded via
Run multiple uvicorn workers with Gunicorn as the process manager for multi-core utilisation:
. Each worker has its own event loop and Python interpreter; shared state must live in Redis or a database, not in-process.

In I/O-bound benchmarks (TechEmpower Framework Benchmarks), FastAPI/Uvicorn consistently matches Node.js Express and outperforms Flask+Gunicorn by 5–10x for JSON responses under high concurrency. The gap narrows when the workload becomes CPU-bound.
installs uvloop on Linux, replacing the default asyncio event loop with a C implementation ~2x faster.
instead of uvicorn for HTTP/2 multiplexing on long-lived LLM streaming connections.
maintains a pool of HTTP/1.1 keep-alive connections; avoids TCP handshake overhead per LLM call.
— for deterministic agent calls, cache results in Redis using
with TTL matching LLM response freshness requirements.
— ASGI server, the default for development and production.
— multi-process production deployment with graceful restarts.
— async ORM sessions; works natively with FastAPI dependency injection.
— database migration tool compatible with SQLAlchemy models.
— rate limiting using Redis sorted sets.
— LangChain's FastAPI-based server for deploying chains as REST endpoints with streaming.
— load config from env vars into a validated
Low-level ASGI; FastAPI builds on it
Alternative to FastAPI with stricter typing
. For agent APIs, API key validation via a
function is the most common pattern — inject the key check into every protected router.
Input validation as a security layer

Pydantic validation is your first line of defence against injection via tool inputs. Constrain field lengths, allowed characters, and value ranges at the model level. Never pass raw user strings directly into shell commands, SQL queries, or LLM system prompts without sanitisation.
to restrict which origins can call your agent API. In production, never set
unless the API is fully public and unauthenticated.
to prevent handlers from hanging indefinitely on slow LLM responses.
retry logic; back off exponentially on 429/503.
— lifespan teardown lets in-flight requests finish before the process exits; combine with a load balancer health check to drain before restart.
— generate a UUID per request in middleware; include in outbound headers (
(readiness) so Kubernetes probes can detect unhealthy pods.

Think of a FastAPI app as a typed function registry. You register Python functions annotated with Pydantic types; FastAPI generates the HTTP contract from those types and wires the web layer to your functions automatically. The ASGI event loop is a single-threaded scheduler: every

is a handoff point where another request can make progress. Keep handlers thin (validate, call service, return), push business logic into injected services, and treat the event loop as cooperative — never monopolise it with CPU work.
FastAPI is the default Python framework for agentic AI HTTP APIs: async-native, Pydantic-validated, auto-documented.
Built on Starlette (ASGI) and Pydantic; run with Uvicorn for single-process, Gunicorn+UvicornWorker for multi-core.
to manage shared async resources (HTTP clients, DB pools) instead of module-level globals.
Every blocking call (sync I/O, CPU work) inside an async handler degrades all concurrent requests; use
Pydantic models are your schema, validator, and serialiser — always declare
composes auth, config, and shared clients cleanly without passing them as function arguments.
wrapping an async generator that yields SSE-formatted lines.
Secure by default: validate all inputs with Pydantic field constraints, authenticate via dependency, add CORS and rate limiting middleware.
